coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of matrix-free carbon dots for information encryption, fingerprinting, and antibacterial activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Ashok
Kumari, Rinki
Behuria, Himadri Gourav
Sahu, Santosh Kumar
Sahu, Sumanta Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Matrix-free room-temperature phosphorescent nanomaterials have received great attention because of their applications in information encryption, optoelectronics, fingerprinting, bioimaging, and sensing. Therefore, we report matrix-free room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots via controlled growth techniques followed by hydrothermal treatment using pyromellitic acid and urea. Two forms of carbon dots have obtained in single reaction vessels, one in aggregated and the other in solution state. The aggregated state green-phosphorescence carbon dots (GP-CDs) showed fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions at 452 nm and 511 nm, respectively. However, the remaining liquid-state carbon dots (L-CDs) displayed fluorescence only. The result illustrated that an ordered and self-protective structure has formed in GP-CDs, and this type of order has not achieved in L-CDs. The compacted and more ordered structures stabilize and induce the efficient generation of a triplet state, which is responsible for a room-temperature phosphorescent generation. Further, GP-CDs have been applied in fingerprint detection and data authentication as they showed dual mode emissions. Interestingly, the L-CDs possess − 17 mV zeta potential and exhibited potential antibacterial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Information encryption
Room-temperature phosphorescent
Antibacterial activity

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of latent fingerprints (LFPs) using GP-CDs
Disc diffusion assay to determine the antimicrobial activity of L-CDs
Evaluation of half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of L-CDs
The L-CDs exhibited membrane lysis as a mechanism to target bacteria

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are very grateful to the Central Research Facility, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad for HR-TEM, FE-SEM, XPS, UV–VIS–NIR, and XRD analysis. A. Kumar is thankful to the Department Research Facility of the Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, for FTIR analysis. We are grateful to Prof. Anindya Dutta's research group, especially Mr. Arkaprava Chowdhury, IIT Bombay, for the measurement of phosphorescence emission. A. Kumar would like to acknowledge the Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, for awarding a research fellowship.

The authors are very grateful to the Central Research Facility, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad for HR-TEM, FE-SEM, XPS, UV–VIS–NIR, and XRD analysis. A. Kumar is thankful to the Department Research Facility of the Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, for FTIR analysis. We are grateful to Prof. Anindya Dutta’s research group, especially Mr. Arkaprava Chowdhury, IIT Bombay, for the measurement of phosphorescence emission. A. Kumar would like to acknowledge the Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, for awarding a research fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-SEM images (a-b), TEM image (c), Particle size histograms (d), XRD pattern (e), UV-Vis absorption spectrum (f), PL emission spectrum (g), Normalized PL Excitation and Emission spectrum (h), Phosphorescent spectra (i) of GP-CDs.
FT-IR spectra (a), XPS survey (b), XPS deconvolution spectrum C1s (c), O1s (d), and N1s (e) of GP-CDs.
Digital images of GP-CDs at different time intervals with UV source ON and OFF (a), digital encryption images of GP-CDs (b).

Digital latent fingerprint images on CD (upper face) (a), CD (glossy face) (b), plastic bottle (c), aluminum foil (d), petry dish (e), and tiffin box (f). Enlarged images of standard LFPs representing bridge (i), crossover (ii), short ridge (iii), bifurcation (iv), loop (v), hook (vi), termination (vii), and dot as an island (viii).
Schematic mechanism of phosphorescent emission in L-CDs and GP-CDs.

Digital images of the zone of inhibition established during disc diffusion method (a), fitting up of OD600 values to Log10[increasing doses] of L-CDs (b), and zone of inhibition for the synthesized L-CDs (c).
Schematic representation of the GP-CDs and L-CDs synthesis.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of matrix-free carbon dots for information encryption, fingerprinting, and antibacterial activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad 826004, Jharkhand, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad 826004, Jharkhand, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, Maharaja Sriram Chandra Bhanjadeo University, Takatpur, Baripada 757003, Odisha, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biotechnology, Maharaja Sriram Chandra Bhanjadeo University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology, Maharaja Sriram Chandra Bhanjadeo University, Takatpur, Baripada, 757003, Odisha, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Matrix-free room-temperature phosphorescent nanomaterials have received great attention because of their applications in information encryption, optoelectronics, fingerprinting, bioimaging, and sensing. Therefore, we report matrix-free room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots via controlled growth techniques followed by hydrothermal treatment using pyromellitic acid and urea. Two forms of carbon dots have obtained in single reaction vessels, one in aggregated and the other in solution state. The aggregated state green-phosphorescence carbon dots (GP-CDs) showed fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions at 452 nm and 511 nm, respectively. However, the remaining liquid-state carbon dots (L-CDs) displayed fluorescence only. The result illustrated that an ordered and self-protective structure has formed in GP-CDs, and this type of order has not achieved in L-CDs. The compacted and more ordered structures stabilize and induce the efficient generation of a triplet state, which is responsible for a room-temperature phosphorescent generation. Further, GP-CDs have been applied in fingerprint detection and data authentication as they showed dual mode emissions. Interestingly, the L-CDs possess − 17 mV zeta potential and exhibited potential antibacterial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Afterglow emission is a fascinating optical phenomenon in which energy is released in the form of photons after removing the external excitation source. Compare with the spontaneous emission attained in fluorescence, the phosphorescent materials emit energy in the form of light longer than 0.1 ms [1]. The materials accomplishing this exciting afterglow property have been used in sensors, LEDs, bioimaging, fingerprint detection, security, and anti-counterfeiting [2–5]. Previously, room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials comprised of rare-earth metals containing inorganic materials, organic compounds, and expensive metal-doped materials [6–8]. Based on the phosphorescence principle, it is quite challenging to comprise the spin-forbidden transition, which causes intersystem crossing (ISC) [9]. To date, several processes have been proposed to restrict vibrational dissipation and promote the ISC. Some feasible strategies are (a) embedding the luminescence materials with solid matrices, (b) crystalline incentive, (c) guest-host doping, (d) H-bonding, (e) H-aggregation, and (f) formation of metal-organic frameworks [10,11]. To date, the available RTP materials generally hold limitations because of their harsh experimental condition, expensive materials used, and blue-emissive phosphorescence emissions. Therefore, it still needed to explore less toxic, environment-friendly, and easily available RTP materials using a facile synthesis route.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new kind of luminescent nanomaterials, have received extensive attention in the field of room-temperature phosphorescence owing to their low toxicity, easily available, tunable emission, and surface-tailoring properties [12]. Notably, CDs showed RTP phenomenon by introducing functional groups (CN and CO) or forming the robust matrix by embedding with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), silica, molten salt, melamine, zeolites, ZnAl2O4, and boric acids. For example, Song et al. have prepared CDs@ZnAl2O4 composites for promoting effective energy transfer between the inorganic trap state and triplet state of CDs [13]. Han et al. have used PVA as a matrix and claimed that PVA rigidifies the oxygen-rich functional groups, which allowed the CDs for phosphorescence generation [14]. Matrix-embedded CDs have an uncontrollable particle size, which limits their applicability in imaging and anticounterfeiting. Recently, RTP exhibiting matrix-free CDs have been accomplished. In order to attain this, researchers have used the heavy atom effect, hydrogen bonding, and controllable reaction conditions. For example, Jiang et al. have reported the matrix-free CDs using succinic acid and diethylenetriamine [15]. On the basis of the self-protection method, Sun et al. have prepared matrix-free carbon dots from benzoic acid derivative and ethylenediamine [16]. In another report, Li et al. used citric acid, urea, and acrylamide as a starting materials for preparing the RTP CDs [17]. The heavy metal atom in CDs is used in producing, populating, amplifying, and activating the triplet state, resulting in phosphorescence [18]. In terms of reaction conditions, prolonged and relatively higher reaction temperature (>250 ºC) is believed to make the efficient triplet generation [19]. It is supposed that the formation of a covalent bond between CDs and organic matrices stabilizes the H-bonding and furthers the triplet state [20]. The synthesis of CDs showing phosphorescence has become an extensive area of research interest and finding their mechanisms is still needed.

Combining all the results, in this work, we report another methodology for preparing scalable matrix-free CDs showing RTP phenomena. The synthesis has made mostly based on three main findings. First, highly abundant nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups (able to produce CN/CO, which favor the n-π * transitions) are selected as the reaction precursors. The nitrogen or oxygen doping favors the population and stabilization of the excited triplet state. Second, reaction conditions especially relatively higher reaction temperature and time have been given because they play an important factor in RTP generations. Third, reaction vessels have been left out even after natural cooling for a certain time for controllable growth/crystallization of particles. In this process, we obtained two kinds of CDs from the same reaction vessel one is in aggregated form and the second one is in solution form, where both the CDs showed fluorescence emissions. Surprisingly, only the aggregate state CDs displayed after-glow properties. The solution state CDs did not exhibit RTP even after freeze-drying or heat drying. It has demonstrated a great example of controlled growth-induced phosphorescence using single reactions. The result presented that an aggregation state forms the self-generated ordered and protective structure in GP-CDs, which is responsible for producing RTP. Further, the GP-CDs fluorescence is applied for fingerprinting and fluorescence-phosphorescence dual-mode emission is used in anti-counterfeiting applications. Interestingly, the developed L-CDs exhibited promising antibacterial activity against two common human pathogenic bacteria: Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (MTCC-1144 & MTCC-3160) and Gram-negative Klebsiella pneumoniae (MTCC-618 & laboratory isolate).

Pyromellitic acid (PA) (>98.0%) was bought from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., LTD., and urea (99.5%) from Merck Specialities Private Limited. Nutrient broth (NB) and antibiotics were purchased from Himedia. Fluorescent lipid analog (NBD-PE) (lyophilized powder) and sodium dithionite were bought from Sigma (India). Molecular grade L-α-phosphatidylcholine from chicken (100 mg/ml in CHCl3) was bought from Avanti Lipids (India). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (≥99%) was purchased from Merck. Each chemical was used exactly how it had been given to us. Millipore water was used during the entire experiment.

The solid UV-Vis absorption spectra were examined using a UV-Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Model: Agilent Cary 5000) and the liquid UV-Vis absorbance spectra were analyzed by a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Model: UV-2600, Shimadzu). The photoluminescence (PL) excitation, emission, and, lifetime decay of solid CDs powder were evaluated using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Model: G9800A, Agilent Carry Eclipse), and the liquid PL excitation and emission spectrum for L-CDs were examined from PerkinElmer model LS 55 spectrophotometer. An instrument namely Cary Eclipse Varian was used for the phosphorescent emission spectra. For the functional group's identification, the Fourier Transformed Infrared (FTIR) spectrum using KBr pellet was analyzed with the help of Agilent Cary 660 instrument. In addition, PHI 5000 Versa Probe III equipment was utilized for X-ray Photoemission Spectroscopy (XPS) in order to examine the surface functionalization. Using a JEM 2100 (JEOL, Japan) microscope, a High-Resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) image of the CDs was acquired. The Supra 55 Zeiss (Germany) field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) was employed to capture an image of the CDs' surface morphologies. The Rigaku Smartlab instrument was used to carry out the High-Resolution X-Ray Diffractometer (HR-XRD) pattern using a Cu Kα X-Ray radiation source. The Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern, UK) was employed for analyzing the zeta potential of CDs, the laser wavelength of 633 nm, and the holder temperature was 25 °C.

The carbon dots were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal synthesis route. At first, 3 mmol of pyromellitic acid and 1 mmol of urea were put into a 50 ml beaker containing 20 ml of Millipore water. Then, we sonicated the mixture for 30 min to disperse the reactant properly. After the sonication time was accomplished, the solution was moved to a 30 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 270˚ C for 12 h. Completing the reaction time, the autoclave was cooled at room temperature (RT) and further left as it is for 24 h. The following day, without any trouble, separate the liquid solution utilizing Whatman filter paper and the solution was freeze-dried. The resulting CDs were named as L-CDs.

Whereas, the inner wall of Teflon contains rod-like solid spikes, which were washed several times with water and centrifuged at 5000 rpm. Further, the resultant product was mixed with 5 ml of water and kept for freeze-drying. Then, the obtained solid was crushed into powder, resulting the GP-CDs. All the drying process was conducted at 100˚ C in a heating oven. A high mass of ∼2 g can be obtained in 100 ml of the reaction mixture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of latent fingerprints (LFPs) using GP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The powdering method was used for latent fingerprint detection and images were taken by a normal mobile phone camera under a UV chamber (365 nm excitation). In this regard, a volunteer rubs his right-hand thumb across his face’s nose and forehead area and then presses the thumb on the various substrates. Hereafter, fine-grinded GP-CDs powder was sprinkled on it and extra powder was removed gently. The different distinctive surfaces like a compact disc (CD) upper face, CD glossy face, plastic bottle, aluminum foil, petri dish, and tiffin box were selected for demonstration purposes during the visualization of LFPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Disc diffusion assay to determine the antimicrobial activity of L-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Antibacterial activity of L-CDs was determined on four different strains of human pathogenic bacteria: S. aureus (MTCC-1144), S. aureus (MTCC-3160), K. pneumoniae (MTCC-618), and K. pneumoniae (laboratory isolate) using agar well diffusion method [21]. Briefly, 50 μg L-CDs from 10 mg/ml stock in DMSO were soaked onto sterilized blotting paper discs of 5 mm diameter and dried completely under sterilized conditions. The disc was placed on NB agar plates spread homogenously with 106 colony-forming units (CFUs) of bacteria grown to the mid-log phase. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and the inhibitory zone's diameter was evaluated. 50 μg/ml of ampicillin was used as a positive control against L-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of L-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of L-CDs on four different strains of human pathogenic bacteria: S. aureus (MTCC-1144), S. aureus (MTCC-3160), K. pneumoniae (MTCC-618), and K. pneumoniae (laboratory isolate) was evaluated by the broth dilution method using microtiter plates [22]. Briefly, the time-dependent growth retardation of bacteria was monitored at 37 °C on the respective bacterial culture treated with 0–0.25 mg/ml of L-CDs. To determine the IC50, 1.6 × 107 bacteria were treated with increasing concentrations of L-CDs in 200 µl NB and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. A JEANWAY 6850 UV–visible spectrophotometer was used to measure the OD600 of the bacteria in order to investigate their growth. The IC50 values of L-CDs for each of the respective bacteria were measured utilizing GraphPad Prism (version 8). The same concentration of ampicillin and DMSO were used as positive and negative control against L-CDs.

The electroformation technique created in our lab was used to make large unilamellar vesicles (LUVs). The trans-bilayer spinning of the fluorescent phospholipid analog NBD-PE in LUVs formed from egg-phosphatidyl choline (egg-PC) was employed to evaluate the lipid flip-flop caused by L-CDs [23]. Fig. S1 provides a schematic representation of the NBD-PE flip-flop induced by the L-CDs. Precisely, Large unilamellar vesicles were cultured at 25 °C in 300 µl of low salt buffer (LSB) (10 mM HEPES, 100 mM NaCl) with increasing doses of L-CDs. By utilizing Agilent JASCO FP-6500 spectrofluorimeter the highly stable initial fluorescence (F0) was measured. To suppress the entire dithionite-accessible NBD-fluorescence (P1 +P2) in preserved LUVs, 10 µl of 1 M sodium dithionite in newly made 1 M Tris-base (pH = 10.5) was added at 1 min and the fluorescence signal was then supervised for 2 min. Where P2 is the extra added fluorescence steady decline in L-CDs treated LUVs and P1 is the fluorescence steady decline in unprocessed controls. This extra added fluorescence steady decline (P2) is because of the L-CDs-induced flipping of NBD-PE from the inner to the outer LUV leaflets. The membranolytic activity (A) of L-CDs is measured using the formula.
A = ([(P1+P2) L-CDs]–[(P1+P2) Control]) /FT ×100
Where, FT = total quenchable fluorescence. FT = F0-FR, where, F0 and FR the initial fluorescence is depicted and FR = remaining fluorescence of LUVs afterward triton-X-100 treatment. GraphPad Prism (version 8) was used to analyze the first-phase (k1) and second-phase (k2) decay rate constants by applying the data to linear and exponential decay functions.

The matrix-free CDs-based RTP processed significant important because of the simple and inexpensive experimental set-up. The uncontrollable growth limits RTP development and its application in forensic science. Whereas, the rigid framework formation retards the molecular motion, which results in crystallization-induced RTP [24,25]. Encouraging this, we provide a new methodology for developing RTP-based CDs by controlling the growth process. Scheme 1 shows the preparation of matrix-free CDs by using pyromellitic acid and urea via hydrothermal treatment. A new method has been proposed to obtain long-lived CDs by providing a longer reaction time and higher reaction temperature using the commonly used precursors. After completion of the reaction, the Teflon container was left over for controllable particle growth, resulting in spike-like structure formation around the wall of the container. The spike-like structure displays dual emission including fluorescence and phosphorescence. Interestingly, the left-over liquid showed only the fluorescence but not the phosphorescence.

To understand the morphology of CDs, FE-SEM and HR-TEM analyses have been performed. FE-SEM images ( Fig. 1 a-b) display GP-CDs have self-aggregated form and size distribution in the nanometer scale. Fig. 1 c shows the CD particle size distribution in a water medium. The HR-TEM confirms the synthesized nanomaterials are spherical in shape and uniformly distributed. Part (d) of Fig. 1 displays the particle size histogram of GP-CDs, which resulted in an average particle size of 4.75 nm. The difference in sizes has been observed using FE-SEM and HR-TEM analysis because of dissimilar sample preparation techniques. The results indicate that the GP-CDs are well-aggregated in solid form, resulting in a larger size. But, the GP-CDs showed a size < 10 nm when it is dispersed in a water medium. The XRD shows a sharp diffraction peak at 27°, which corresponds to the (002) crystalline plane of graphite (Fig. 1 e) [26,27]. It is represented during the controllable growth followed by hydrothermal reaction, decarboxylation, and carbonization steps happened between PA and urea, resulting in a repeating and orderly arrangement of sp2 conjugated graphitic-like structure in GP-CDs.

The optical properties have been examined by UV-Vis absorbance, PL emission/excitation, and phosphorescence spectrum. The UV-Vis absorbance spectra (Fig. 1 f) display peaks at 262 and 295 nm, which are responsible for π–π * transitions of CC groups, and peak at 364 nm for n–π * transitions of CO/CN groups, correspondingly [28]. The GP-CDs have a maximum emission at 452 nm at the optimum excitation wavelength of 380 nm (Fig. 1 g). This illustrates that CO and CN groups present on the GP-CDs surface are responsible for fluorescence emissions. Part (h) of Fig. 1 shows the normalized PL excitation and emission spectra. Surprisingly, the aggregated state GP-CDs showed after-glow emission. Part (i) of Fig. 1 exhibits an emission peak after the excitation source was cut-off. The GP-CDs displayed a green color phosphorescence emission peak at 511 nm under the 380 nm of external source cut-off. The FTIR spectrum further gives the idea of the surface structural information, shown in Fig. 2 a. FTIR spectra of the GP-CDs display distinct peaks at 1690 cm−1, 1611 & 1421 cm−1, 1574 cm−1, and 1284 cm−1 are attributed to stretching of OC-O/OC-N, CC, N-H, and C-N, respectively [15,29–31]. The broad range of peaks within 3100–3500 cm−1 are assigned to the N-H/O-H stretching vibrations [32]. Further, The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis has been performed to know more about the structural features. The XPS survey peaks are observed for C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s at 284.41 eV, 402.07 eV, and 531.15 eV, respectively (Fig. 2 b). The survey scan shows that carbon (64.9%), oxygen (32.8%), and nitrogen (2.3%) are the main constituents of GP-CDs. Furthermore, the high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2 c) is deconvoluted into three peaks at 283.84 eV, 288.02 eV, and 289.28 eV for the C-C/CC, O-C-O/CO, and OC-N/OC-OH, respectively [33]. The high-resolution XPS of the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 d) also deconvoluted into two peaks at 530.97 eV and 532.41 eV illustrating the presence of CO and C-O groups, correspondingly [34,35]. The XPS N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 e) displays two characteristics peak at 398.68 eV and 400.51 eV attributed to pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [36,37]. These results confirm that GP-CDs contain hydroxyl, amine, amide, and carboxyl moieties on their surface. As the CN and CO groups have been acknowledged to facilitate the intersystem crossing (ISC) and stabilize the excited triplet state, which favors the RTP generation [38].

The GP-CDs powder appeared white in color in visible light and turns to blue color at 254 nm and at 365 nm. It is observed that they change to green color after the UV lamp is turned off as shown in Video 1 & Video 2, respectively. This interesting behavior indicates that GP-CDs can be used in the digital encoding and data security field. To know the RTP lifetime, the time-resolved spectra of GP-CDs is measured at 380 nm. Further, the obtained data were double-exponentially fitted on the following Eq. [1]:
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2023.131460.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1
.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2023.131460.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S2.
Video S2
(1)
τ
avg
= A
τ
1
2
+ B
τ
2
2
/
A τ
1
+ B
τ
2
Where A and B are the fitting parameters;
τ
1
and
τ
2
are the different times. The calculated average RTP lifetime is ∼56.36 ms (Fig. S2). The double-exponential decay conveys that GP-CDs have different decay channels. The energy gap between excited singlet and triplet state (ΔST) can be calculated by fluorescence and phosphorescence emission using Planck’s quantum theory [39,40]. The calculated ΔST is 0.32 eV as S1 and T1 is 2.74 eV and 2.42 eV, respectively. The small ΔST is favorable for an efficient intersystem crossing from excited singlet to triplet state. The RTP performance of GP-CDs is collected under 365 and 254 nm using UV lamp. Under both the excitation source cut-off, GP-CDs showed green color RTP. The green color emission stayed for several seconds when the external source cut off and was visually seen by the naked eye ( Fig. 3 a).

Fig. 3(b) displays the information encryption ability of GP-CDs is checked by applying the code “I I I I I I ” in the letter “HITU”. Other joining patterns of code are made with any other fluorescent materials. Under the visible light white colored letter “HITU” are seen, while the encrypted code “ I I I I I I” is perceived for several seconds after turning the UV light off. While the rest of the linking patterns are not attained after the external source cut-off. Such RTP-based encryption is difficult to be copied, thus GP-CDs are showing great potential to be applied as high-level information encryption agents.

To vary the applicability, the GP-CDs have also used in fingerprint detection because their powder was highly fluorescent in nature. For the LFPs analysis, the powdering method was employed [41]. There are various substrates like a compact disc (CD) upper face, CD glossy face, plastic bottle, aluminum foil, petry dish, and tiffin box were chosen for latent fingerprint detection. It is worth noticing that GP-CDs have able to exhibit even the three levels of patterns. Fig. 4(a-f) displayed the first-level features including patterns or bulges. Further, for a high level of patterning, a high-resolution image of a CD (glossy face) was selected as a standard substrate. The second level comprises more remarkable details of the fingerprint which contains bifurcation and termination, as shown in Fig. 4(iv and vii). We also obtained the third level of patterning details including bridge, crossover, short ridge, loop, hook, and dot as an island, displayed in Fig. 4(i, ii, iii, v, vi, and, viii). The outstanding fluorescence patterning of GP-CDs represents itself as an attractive candidate for LFPs applications.

For L-CDs, the HR-TEM image displays the well-dispersion of the particles and the particles are spherical in shape (Fig. S3a). Part (b) of Fig. S3 displays the histogram, which represents the average particle size of ∼3.35 nm. In comparison to the GP-CDs, the L-CDs have a blue-shifted absorption band. The absorbance spectra of L-CDs (Fig. S3c) displayed only peaks ≤ 300 nm, which are accountable for π–π * transitions of CC groups [42]. The fluorescence emission of L-CDs showed a similar emission maximum, where the peak appeared at 450 nm under the excitation of 340 nm (Fig. S3d). The normalized PLEx and PLEm of L-CDs are displayed in Fig. S3e. Part (f) of Fig. S3 shows L-CDs have a zeta potential of – 17 mV, negatively charged might be forming due to -COO− and a small amount of -NH2 groups present on their surface. Fig. S4 displays the L-CDs have OC-O/OC-N, CC, N-H, and C-N functionality on their surface, which is similar to the GP-CDs.

For GP-CDs, results suggest that at higher temperatures and pressure, carboxyl moieties of PA made the cross-linked long-chain network with the amine of urea, which helps in the formation of regular and ordered-CDs structures. The hydrothermal reaction was processed at temperatures of 230, 250, and 270 ºC, and the best RTP phenomenon is obtained at T = 270 ºC. Further, the RTP formation of CDs was also analyzed by fixing the reaction temperature and varying the reaction conditions including precursors ratio and time. The variable reaction time was 8, 12, and 20 h; the concentration ratio of PA: urea was 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, 2:1, and 3:1. The best result of RTP was obtained at a specific ratio of 3:1 and reaction duration of 12 h. Fig. S5 demonstrates the RTP images of CDs, which was synthesized with a precursors ratio of (3:1) at different reaction temperature (230, 250, and 270 ºC) for 12 h. It represents that as the reaction temperature increases, carbonization occurs and a more compacted structure may form via hydrogen bonding. Previously reported works of literature also presented the formation of a higher degree of crystallinity in the CDs interior by enhancing the reaction temperature (>250 ºC) [43]. It is verified by plotting the XRD of CDs synthesized at three different temperatures, where Fig. S6 displays the higher degree of crystallinity of CDs synthesized at T = 270 ºC. This shows that comparatively, a higher reaction temperature is critical for the matrix-free RTP formation in CDs. Generally, it has been seen that the RTP phenomenon has been observed in high-crystalline form or by providing a robust structure by embedding with the specific matrix [44]. In this work, it is representing that offering the precise reaction condition favors the formation of stabilized T1 state due to higher-order of crystallinity in GP-CDs without using any matrices. The aromatic chromophores in the aggregate state form an ordered π-π stacking structure, which also induces the extent of conjugation [45]. In the aggregate state, conjugated π-π stacking provides a self-protective structure. The higher extent of bonding, compact assembly in aggregated states, and more ordered structure induce the efficient generation of a triplet state. In this case, the triplet state generation, and energy transfer are favorable. It has resulted that the slow rate of particle growth provides a greater ordered structure and efficient RTP in GP-CDs. However, this type of order can’t be obtained in the solution state due to more randomness of particles. Therefore, L-CDs are unable to exhibit RTP emission. Even coming the L-CDs into solid form, they are unable to provide the after-glow emission. It is representing that L-CDs do not form the ordered or compacted structure in solution as well as in the aggregate state. This also indicates that the RTP performance of GP-CDs powder does not instigate by aggregation only, rather than this, RTP initiates from the GP-CDs itself. Fig. 5 displays the plausible structure-related RTP mechanism in (a) L-CDs and (b) GP-CDs.

The L-CDs exhibited antimicrobial activity against S. aureus (MTCC 1144), S. aureus (MTCC 3160), K. pneumoniae (MTCC 618), and K. pneumoniae (laboratory isolate) as indicated by their zone of inhibition. All four strains displayed sensitivity towards L-CDs as designated by their zone of inhibition which was comparable to the zone of inhibition displayed by the current generation of the membranotropic drug: ampicillin ( Fig. 6 a). The 0.25 mg/ml L-CDs in the NB agar plate exhibited the highest zone of inhibition against S. aureus (MTCC 1144) and the lowest zone of inhibition against the K. pneumoniae laboratory isolate.

The K. pneumoniae strain isolated in our laboratory is a heavy metal tolerant isolate that exhibits tolerance against 300 mM HgCl2 in a growth medium. As heavy metal resistance in microbes provides cross-resistance against antibiotics, the observed low sensitivity of K. pneumoniae towards L-CDs might have resulted from nanoparticle-induced co-expression of genes related to Hg2+ resistance. The presence of up to 0.25 mg/ml L-CDs in a growth medium led to growth reduction in all four bacterial strains at 24 h of incubation as indicated by a sharp decline in their respective OD600 values. Fitting up of OD600 values to Log10[increasing doses] of L-CDs shows that the effective concentration of carbon dots that leads to 50% growth reduction in bacteria (IC50) is from 80 to 105 μg (Fig. 6 b). S. aureus (MTCC 1144), S. aureus (MTCC 3160), K. pneumoniae (MTCC 618), and K. pneumoniae exhibited half minimal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of 80–105 μg/ml for L-CDs (Fig. 6 c). The observed variation in IC50 values indicates either their differential membrane permeability into bacteria or their differential bacteria-specific resistance mechanism. The L-CDs exhibited strong antibacterial activity against all the strains comparable to ampicillin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The L-CDs exhibited membrane lysis as a mechanism to target bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: LUVs are dependable in-vitro membrane model systems which used to start investigating events connected to membranes. By using copper electrodes as well as the electroformation method, LUVs were developed from 100 μg (130 nmol) egg-PC producing 200–2500 LUVs/ml. By suppressing the fluorescent PL analog NBD-PE that was restricted to the outer membrane leaflet of LUVs by the membrane-impermeable component sodium dithionite, the membrane leakage activity of L-CDs was examined. When LUVs with 1% NBD-PE were incubated without or with L-CDs, they showed a normalized stable fluorescence of 100% (F0) (Fig. S1). Sodium dithionite treatment resulted in the immediate quenching of NBD-PEs in the outer leaflet while leaving the inner leaflet-specific NBD-PE unaffected. This dithionite quenching of NBD fluorescence decreased the early fluorescence by about (50 ± 1.05) % demonstrating its unilamellar. However, treatment of LUVs with 100 and 200 µg L-CDs led to a 60% and 70% reduction in NBD-fluorescence upon dithionite addition. This additional reduction in fluorescence L-CDs treated LUVs was due to the L-CDs induced lysis of LUV membranes leading to the leakage of dithionite into the LUV lumens (Fig. S7). These results indicate that incubation of L-CDs with LUVs leads to pore formation in the lipid bilayer that allows the water-soluble membrane impermeable sodium dithionite to quench inner fluorescence NBD-PE. Additionally, the addition of triton-X-100 leads to whole membrane lysis by quenching of all fluorescent lipids, indicating that the complete lysis of LUVs by triton-X-100 leads to a 100% reduction of NBD-fluorescence.

In summary, we developed matrix-free RTP CDs using PA and urea with a combination of hydrothermal treatment and controlled growth. The results concluded that precise reaction conditions favor the formation of populated and stabilized T1 states due to higher order of crystallinity in GP-CDs. Since GP-CDs exhibit dual-mode emissions, they have applied in fingerprint detection and advanced anti-counterfeiting. Additionally, the L-CDs exhibit a high antibacterial activity on human pathogenic strains of S. aureus and K. pneumoniae that is comparable to the commercially available synthetic antibiotic ampicillin. The mechanism adopted by the L-CDs is bactericidal with an IC50 value of 80–105 μM. The L-CDs exhibit significant lysis of LUVs, indicating that they use bacterial membrane lysis as a plausible mechanism to exhibit their antibacterial activity. It represents that L-CDs could be a potent alternative to synthetic commercial antibiotics against bacteria. This works not only offers a facile and scalable approach for preparing RTP CDs, but also exhibits specified applications in anti-counterfeiting, fingerprinting, and antibacterial activity.

Ashok Kumar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Rinki Kumari: Formal analysis, Investigation. Himadri Gourav Behuria: Validation. Santosh Kumar Sahu: Validation, Investigation. Sumanta Kumar Sahu: Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Sumanta kumar sahu reports financial support was provided by IIT (ISM) Dhanbad.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful to the Central Research Facility, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad for HR-TEM, FE-SEM, XPS, UV–VIS–NIR, and XRD analysis. A. Kumar is thankful to the Department Research Facility of the Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, for FTIR analysis. We are grateful to Prof. Anindya Dutta’s research group, especially Mr. Arkaprava Chowdhury, IIT Bombay, for the measurement of phosphorescence emission. A. Kumar would like to acknowledge the Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, for awarding a research fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2023.131460.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
